title: Cross Section Parallel to the Base

steps.instruction: A cross section is the shape you see when you slice through a 3D figure with a flat plane.
steps.work: Think of slicing a loaf of bread — each slice shows a cross section.
steps.explanation: The shape of the cross section depends on the 3D figure and the angle of the cut.

steps.instruction: If you slice a cylinder parallel to its circular base, the cross section is a circle.
steps.explanation: Cutting parallel to the base always produces a shape similar to the base.

steps.instruction: A rectangular prism cut parallel to its base gives a rectangle. A triangular prism gives a triangle.
steps.work: Rectangular prism → rectangle. Triangular prism → triangle.
steps.explanation: The cross section matches the base shape when the cut is parallel to it.

title: Cross Section Perpendicular to the Base

steps.instruction: Cutting perpendicular to the base means slicing straight down through the figure.
steps.work: Imagine cutting a can (cylinder) straight down the middle from top to bottom.
steps.explanation: Perpendicular cuts produce different shapes than parallel cuts.

steps.instruction: A vertical cut through the center of a cylinder produces a rectangle.
steps.work: The rectangle has height = cylinder height and width = diameter.
steps.explanation: The cut reveals the full height and the width across the circular base.

steps.instruction: A vertical cut through the center of a cone (passing through the apex) produces a triangle.
steps.work: The triangle has height = cone height and base = diameter.
steps.explanation: The cut through the apex of a cone reveals its triangular profile.
